P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Mr. Richard P. Staley 
City of Thousand Oaks 
P.O. Box 1496 
Thousand Oaks, CA 
91360 
Dear Mr. Staley: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
April 21, 1982 
Enfarcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Intere 
322-6444 
This is in reply to your letter dated April 13, 1982, regarding the 
method of disclosing the receipt of donated items, and the subsequent 
receipt of proceeds from the sale of such items at an auction or 
garage sale. 
At the time the items are received, the candidate has received an 
"in-kind" or "non-monetary" contribution in the amount of the fair 
market value of the item. 
When the item is sold, if the amount received is the fair market 
value, or less than the fair market value, that amount is reported 
as a "miscellaneous adjustment of cash" on Schedule G. 
It is not 
reported as a contribution because the purchaser has received full 
and adequante consideration for the payment. 
In the event an amount substantially greater than the fair market 
value is received for an item, that amount over the fair market 
value is reported as a monetary contribution on Schedule A since, 
presumably, the purchaser intended to make a contribution to the 
campaign, and did not receive full and adequate consideration for 
the payment. 
Because the value of in-kind contributions is not added into the 
cash on hand, this method of reporting will accurately reflect 
the committee's financial condition. 
I hope this adequately answers your questions. 
further questions, please contact me. 
If you have any 
JP:c:n 
Sincerely,

401 WEST HILLCREST DRIVE' POST OFFICE SOX 1496 . THOUSAND OAKS, CALIFORNIA 91360 . (AREA 805)497-8611 
City Attorney 
April 13, 1982 
Ms. Jean Pritchard 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Ms. Pritchard: 
Pursuant to our recent telephone conversation, I am writing this 
letter to seek your written guidance regarding campaign disclosure 
requirements. 
The question regards the method of disclosing the receipt and 
disposal of items donated to a candidate. 
For purposes of ease of 
description, I will refer to them as "garage sale" items. 
The 
question I pose is how to disclose the receipt of such items and to 
disclose the proceeds from the sale of such items. 
The situation 
would involve items being donated to a candidate who would then hold 
an auction or other sale and dispose of the goods. 
For purposes of dealing with this issue in a broader context, 
please indicate how the items would be disclosed if (1) the amount of 
money received for the goods bears some relation to its probable value, 
or (2) the amount of money received is either less or more than the 
probable value of the item. 
Your attention to this matter is greatly appreciated. 
If any 
further issues need to be identified, please do not hesitate to contact 
me. 
RPS:jt 
Sincerely yours, 
RICHARD P. STALEY 
Acting City Attorney

sections.conclusion: At the time the items are received, the candidate has received an "in-kind" or "non-monetary" contribution in the amount of the fair market value of the item. When the item is sold, if the amount received is the fair market value, or less than the fair market value, that amount is reported as a "miscellaneous adjustment of cash" on Schedule G. It is not reported as a contribution because the purchaser has received full and adequate consideration for the payment. In the event an amount substantially greater than the fair market value is received for an item, that amount over the fair market value is reported as a monetary contribution on Schedule A since, presumably, the purchaser intended to make a contribution to the campaign, and did not receive full and adequate consideration for the payment. Because the value of in-kind contributions is not added into the cash on hand, this method of reporting will accurately reflect the committee's financial condition.
sections.question_synthetic: How should a candidate disclose the receipt of in-kind donated items and the subsequent proceeds from selling those items, with variations based on whether the sale price matches, exceeds, or falls below the fair market value?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: When in-kind donated items are received, they must be reported as non-monetary contributions at fair market value. When sold, proceeds up to or below fair market value are reported as miscellaneous cash adjustments on Schedule G, while any amount exceeding fair market value is reported as a monetary contribution on Schedule A, reflecting the purchaser's intent to contribute.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the FPPC response letter (dated April 21, 1982 from Jeanne Pritchard) and the incoming request letter (dated April 13, 1982 from Richard P. Staley). OCR quality varies between sections; some sections show handwritten signatures and editorial marks. Extracted question and conclusion from clearly legible portions.

disclose the proceeds from the sale of such items. 
The situation 
would involve items being donated to a candidate who would then hold 
an auction or other sale and dispose of the goods. 
For purposes of dealing with this issue in a broader context, 
please indicate how the items would be disclosed if (1) the amount of 
money received for the goods bears some relation to its probable value,

RICHARD P. STALEY

CONCLUSION: At the time the items are received, the candidate has received an "in-kind" or "non-monetary" contribution in the amount of the fair market value of the item. When the item is sold, if the amount received is the fair market value, or less than the fair market value, that amount is reported as a "miscellaneous adjustment of cash" on Schedule G. It is not reported as a contribution because the purchaser has received full and adequate consideration for the payment. In the event an amount substantially greater than the fair market value is received for an item, that amount over the fair market value is reported as a monetary contribution on Schedule A since, presumably, the purchaser intended to make a contribution to the campaign, and did not receive full and adequate consideration for the payment. Because the value of in-kind contributions is not added into the cash on hand, this method of reporting will accurately reflect the committee's financial condition.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Mr. Richard P. Staley on proper disclosure methods for in-kind donated items and their subsequent sale proceeds, specifying different reporting requirements based on whether sale amounts match, fall below, or exceed the fair market value of the items.
